questions.question: What is the fundamental reason a CDN reduces latency for distant users?
options: It compresses files more than the origin can
It serves content from a cache physically near the user, avoiding a long round trip across the globe
It runs the application faster than the origin server
It uses a faster version of HTTP only available to CDNs
questions.explanation: Latency is bounded by physics — a cross-continent round trip is ~150ms of pure transit. A CDN can't make light faster, so it moves copies of the content to edge locations near users, turning a transcontinental trip into a few-millisecond local fetch.

questions.question: After you set a one-year max-age on a file, how do you ship an update without waiting a year?
options: You can't — you must wait for the year to pass
Give the new version a new URL (cache busting via content-hashed filenames), so caches never serve the old one
Restart all the CDN servers manually
Reduce max-age to zero retroactively on cached copies
questions.explanation: The standard pattern is cache busting: fingerprint asset filenames with a content hash (app.3f9a2c.js). A new build produces a new filename/URL the CDN has never cached, and the HTML points to it. Old URLs are simply never requested again, sidestepping invalidation.

questions.question: How does anycast route a user to the nearest CDN PoP?
options: It measures every user's GPS location
The same IP address is advertised from every PoP, and internet routing naturally delivers each user's packets to the closest one
Each user manually selects a PoP
It assigns a unique IP per user
questions.explanation: With anycast, all PoPs announce the same IP address. The internet's routing then directs each user's traffic to the topologically nearest PoP advertising that address — no per-user configuration needed.

questions.question: What happens on a CDN cache MISS at an edge PoP?
options: The request fails
The PoP fetches from the origin, caches the result per the cache rules, and serves it — so the next user in that region gets a hit
The user is redirected to the origin permanently
The CDN deletes the content everywhere
questions.explanation: On a miss, the edge fetches the content from the origin (your servers or object store), stores it according to the response's cache headers, and returns it. The first request to a region pays the long trip; everyone after rides the now-warm edge cache.

questions.question: A team sets `Cache-Control: public, max-age=600` on an endpoint that returns the logged-in user's profile. What's the danger?
options: Nothing — this is correct
A shared cache (CDN) may store one user's private profile and serve it to other users; personal responses need no-store or private
The profile will load too slowly
The CDN will refuse to cache it
questions.explanation: `public` allows shared caches like the CDN to store the response. For per-user data, a cached copy can be served to the wrong user — a serious data-leak bug. Personalized responses should use `no-store` (don't cache) or `private` (browser only, never the CDN).
